content.text:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user invoked `/softlight:softlight` with the request: "I am working on the billing app. We have organizations and users, and I want to get a quick feel for how much organization usage there is, and how that is split between users. help me find a solution to that."
   
   This triggered the full Softlight designer codegen pipeline: clone the billing app, explore the codebase, screenshot the current experience, frame the design problem with multiple framings, create explorations on a canvas, dispatch a presenter agent, and dispatch prototype agents until every prototype is generated.
   
   The explicit project setup: project_id=297a25a6-8deb-4223-a9ec-f6cd2a950550, URL at https://softlight.orianna.ai/projects/297a25a6-8deb-4223-a9ec-f6cd2a950550.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Softlight design pipeline (clone-app-codegen → present-canvas → generate-prototype subagents)
   - Bazel build system (bazel run //server/example pattern)
   - AG-Grid (React data grids with tree data/master-detail capabilities)
   - Radix UI Themes (dark theme, Card, Progress, Badge, Dialog)
   - FastAPI + asyncpg (billing service backend)
   - Stripe integration (customers, subscriptions, meters, webhooks)
   - React Query (@tanstack/react-query for data fetching)
   - Treemap visualization (d3 squarify)
   - Stacked area charts (recharts/visx)
   - MCP (Model Context Protocol) over HTTP at https://softlight.orianna.ai/mcp/
   - Drive upload pattern: curl -sF 'file=@-;filename=spec.json' https://drive.orianna.ai/api/v2/upload

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - /workspaces/orianna/server/billing/ui/App.tsx
     - Main billing admin app with 7 tabs: Accounts, Subscriptions, Prices, Upgrades, Quotas, Usage Events, Usages
     - Uses Radix Tabs + AG-Grid with ThemeQuartz dark theme
   - /workspaces/orianna/server/billing/service/schema/schema.sql
     - accounts table (id, domains, members TEXT[], name, owner, priority, stripe_customer_id)
     - usage_events (id, email, metric, subscription_id) — per-user attribution lives here
     - subscriptions, prices, quotas, upgrades tables
   - /workspaces/orianna/server/billing/api/usage.py
     - Usage model: account, billing_period, metric, price, quota, subscription, usage count
   - /workspaces/orianna/server/billing/api/metric.py
     - Two metrics: projects_created, revisions_generated
   - /workspaces/orianna/server/billing/service/routes.py
     - /api/usages endpoint returns aggregate usage per (account × subscription × metric × period)
     - /api/usage-events endpoint returns per-user events
   - /workspaces/orianna/server/billing/service/meters.py
     - active_softlight_users_v1 meter counts unique emails per subscription
   - /workspaces/orianna/server/billing/ui/components/tables/UsageTable.tsx
     - Current Usages tab: flat AG-Grid with Account, Subscription, Price, Quota, Metric, Soft/Hard Limit, Usage, Usage %, Starts At, Ends At
   - /workspaces/orianna/server/billing/ui/components/tables/UsageEventTable.tsx
     - Usage Events tab: flat grid with Created At, ID, Subscription, Email, Metric
   - /workspaces/orianna/server/billing/ui/components/editors/AccountEditor.tsx
     - Current "click account" behavior: opens a plain form editor dialog for CRUD, no usage view
   - /workspaces/orianna/.mcp.json
     - softlight MCP server configured at https://softlight.orianna.ai/mcp/ (HTTP type)
   - /workspaces/orianna/infrastructure/ts/icons/ (57 available icons including ArrowUpIcon, ArrowDownIcon, ExternalLinkIcon, EllipsisIcon, ChevronRight, X)
   - Uploaded specs (6 of 9):
     - Spec 1.1 Leaderboard cards: https://drive.orianna.ai/46035d690d0ed4e0649c62515fc91c5f.json
     - Spec 1.2 Treemap: https://drive.orianna.ai/38675ff3b607e6a9045cb6141b75916a.json
     - Spec 1.3 Time-first stacked area: https://drive.orianna.ai/d5326e3e58cbfe040514691cee0a6fec.json
     - Spec 2.1 Grouped tree: https://drive.orianna.ai/35884d254f13717a9f8d6125bf21b57b.json
     - Spec 2.2 Side panel: https://drive.orianna.ai/7540c47ce86317e81a2d0e9aa4b9bab3.json
     - Spec 2.3 Inline stacked bar: https://drive.orianna.ai/6e4c32fdaebe12c0116ea6c969aadf62.json

4. Errors and fixes:
   - Softlight MCP tools disappeared mid-session (no longer in deferred tools list):
     - Fixed by dispatching subagent that directly called the HTTP MCP endpoint at https://softlight.orianna.ai/mcp/
     - Subagent discovered actual schema: create_exploration uses `count` (not `num_slots`), does not accept `description` parameter
   - Present-canvas agent (id a7b15aa8698ec143b) failed for same reason — returned detailed layout plan but could not execute since `get_project`, `move_slots`, `create_text_element` were not in its tool list. Needs re-dispatch with explicit instructions to use HTTP MCP.
   - browser_wait_for `time` parameter expected number, I passed string — fixed by using numeric value
   - Navigating directly to ?tab=usages URL didn't persist tab state — fixed by clicking tab via browser_click

5. Problem Solving:
   - **Framing work committed (3 genuinely different framings):**
     - A: No at-a-glance surface → Build a new Overview dashboard
     - B: Usages tab has the data but flat → Restructure for roll-up/drill-down inside grid
     - C: Wrong unit of question → Build per-org detail page
   - **3 explorations created on canvas** with slot/caption IDs captured (see section 3)
   - **Clone completed:** baseline_dir=/tmp/billing-clone, port=5182, tunnel_id=28a441b9-81d6-4cd5-9221-145ab0a2dcc3
   - **Mock data consolidated for all prototypes** using baseline's 6 orgs:
     - Stark Industries: 4,620/5,000 revs (92% near limit). Top users: tony@stark.com (47%), pepper@stark.com (29%), happy@stark.com (14%)
     - Initech Solutions: 3,180/5,000 (64%). Top users: bill, peter, samir, michael
     - Wayne Enterprises: 2,850/5,000 (57%). Top users: bruce, alfred, lucius
     - Acme Corp: 1,420/5,000 (28%). Top users: alice, bob, carol
     - Globex Industries: 890/5,000 (18%, Free)
     - Umbrella Research: 210/5,000 (4%, Free)

6. All user messages:
   - Initial user command: `/softlight:softlight` with args "I am working on the billing app. We have organizations and users, and I want to get a quick feel for how much organization usage there is, and how that is split between users. help me find a solution to that."
   - Second user message triggered the run-designer-codegen skill: "You are an agent working on Softlight project 297a25a6-8deb-4223-a9ec-f6cd2a950550. You are running in a non-interactive environment. Do not ask the user questions - they have no way to answer. Invoke the `run-designer-codegen` skill to create the project and generate the initial explorations. Do not stop until you have generated every prototype in every exploration that you create in the project."
   - Multiple system reminders about TodoWrite use
   - Task notifications about clone agent completing and present-canvas agent completing (with failure)

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Upload 3 remaining specs: 3.1 (Stripe-style KPI cards), 3.2 (Chart leads), 3.3 (Two-column split)
   - Re-dispatch present-canvas agent with explicit instructions to use HTTP MCP endpoint directly (the way the first subagent did)
   - Dispatch 9 generate-prototype subagents in parallel (one per slot), passing: project_id, slot_id, caption_slot_id, baseline_dir=/tmp/billing-clone, spec_url, images (3 baseline screenshots), and app context
   - Validate every slot_id has an iframe element (not placeholder) by calling get_project; retry any that failed

8. Current Work:
   Immediately before the summary request, I was in the middle of uploading the 9 design specs to drive. I had already uploaded specs 1.1 through 2.3 (6 specs) in two batches. The last batch of parallel uploads returned these URLs:
   - Spec 1.2: https://drive.orianna.ai/38675ff3b607e6a9045cb6141b75916a.json
   - Spec 1.3: https://drive.orianna.ai/d5326e3e58cbfe040514691cee0a6fec.json
   - Spec 2.1: https://drive.orianna.ai/35884d254f13717a9f8d6125bf21b57b.json
   - Spec 2.2: https://drive.orianna.ai/7540c47ce86317e81a2d0e9aa4b9bab3.json
   - Spec 2.3: https://drive.orianna.ai/6e4c32fdaebe12c0116ea6c969aadf62.json
   
   The todos show "Create explorations and dispatch presenter" as in_progress. Still pending: upload specs 3.1/3.2/3.3, re-dispatch present-canvas, dispatch 9 prototype agents, validate.
   
   The 3 remaining specs for Exploration 3 (Organization detail page) need to cover:
   - 3.1 Stripe-style overview with KPI cards row, trend chart, ranked user table, subscriptions section
   - 3.2 Chart leads — giant stacked-by-user usage chart is hero, users contextual
   - 3.3 Two-column split — left is usage (chart, quota), right is people (ranked members with per-user stats)

9. Optional Next Step:
   Continue directly with the immediately-next step of the pipeline: upload the 3 remaining specs (3.1, 3.2, 3.3 for the Organization detail page exploration) to drive, then re-dispatch the present-canvas agent with explicit HTTP MCP instructions and dispatch all 9 generate-prototype subagents in parallel.
   
   Specifically, the user's explicit directive was: "Do not stop until you have generated every prototype in every exploration that you create in the project." The work was actively uploading specs for prototype dispatch. The next parallel batch should upload specs 3.1, 3.2, 3.3, then dispatch all 9 prototype agents with their respective slot_ids, caption_slot_ids, baseline_dir=/tmp/billing-clone, spec_urls, and baseline screenshot URLs (https://drive.orianna.ai/6078bd8a196d59f9800801aa25937f2f.png, https://drive.orianna.ai/394527d04b668ce8db157fd1a41c10ae.png, https://drive.orianna.ai/23bea46209f91c5a86b980fdab8e5cf7.png).

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /home/vscode/.claude/projects/-workspaces-orianna/e1db3943-2743-4bc7-afed-d7ec13fc6dee.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

input.command:
# Extract all slots with their types, titles, and structure
python3 <<'PY'
import json, re

with open('/tmp/project_state.txt') as f:
    raw = f.read()

# Strip SSE envelope
data_line = [l for l in raw.split('\n') if l.startswith('data: ')]
payload = json.loads(data_line[0][6:])
text = payload['result']['content'][0]['text']
obj = json.loads(text)

revisions = obj['project']['revisions']
print(f"# revisions: {len(revisions)}")
for rev in revisions:
    print(f"\n-- rev {rev['metadata']['id']}")
    slots = rev['slots']
    # Sort by y then x
    slots_sorted = sorted(slots, key=lambda s: (s['y'], s['x']))
    for s in slots_sorted:
        el = s['element']
        t = el['type']
        desc = ''
        if t == 'text':
            desc = f"[{el.get('variant','')}] bold={el.get('bold')} text={el.get('text','')[:80]!r}"
        elif t == 'placeholder':
            desc = f"ph={el.get('content_type')}"
            refs = s.get('references', [])
            if refs:
                desc += f" refs={[r['slot_id'][:8] for r in refs]}"
        elif t == 'iframe':
            desc = f"tunnel={el.get('tunnel_id','')}"
        print(f"  {s['metadata']['id']}  x={s['x']:>5.0f} y={s['y']:>5.0f} w={s['width']:>5.0f} h={s['height']:>5.0f}  {t:12s} {desc}")
PY

input.command:
curl -sF 'file=@-;filename=spec-3-1.json' https://drive.orianna.ai/api/v2/upload <<'EOF'
{"spec": "Add a dedicated organization detail page, styled as a Stripe-like single-page dashboard. Clicking any Account row in the Accounts tab opens this page for that org (instead of the current edit dialog). Dataset and layout follow.

## Entry / navigation
- In the Accounts tab, row clicks navigate to the org detail page inline (swap the tab's content from the AG-Grid to the detail view). Keep the existing tab bar visible at the top.
- The detail view header has:
  - Back button on the far left: a small ghost button with a LeftArrow icon and the label 'All accounts'. Clicking it returns to the Accounts grid.
  - Org name in H2 (e.g. 'Stark Industries'). Below it, a small gray line: 'stark.com · 3 members · Created Jan 14, 2026'.
  - Right side: a Badge showing plan ('Pro', color=accent) and a ghost 'Edit' button that opens the existing AccountEditor dialog.
- Body below is a vertically stacked list of sections, all inside a max-width 1400px centered container with 32px vertical rhythm between sections.

## Section 1: KPI cards row (top of body)
A single row of exactly 4 equal-width Radix Card components, gap=16px.
- Card 1 'Revisions this period': giant number 4,620 (H2, 32px, bold). Below: a small muted label 'of 5,000 limit · resets Nov 30'. Tiny horizontal progress bar underneath (Radix Progress, value=92, color=orange — 80-95% is 'approaching' orange, above 95% is red). At the right edge of the card, vertically centered: a pill Badge 'Near limit' in orange with an AlertTriangle icon.
- Card 2 'Projects this period': number 186. Muted subtitle: '+24 vs last period'. A tiny ArrowUp icon inline with the +24 in green.
- Card 3 'Active users': number 3. Muted subtitle '3 of 3 seats used'. Tiny face-pile of 3 avatars (colored circles with initials TS, PP, HH) at the bottom-right corner of the card.
- Card 4 'MRR': number '$249'. Muted subtitle 'Pro plan · monthly'. Small 'Manage in Stripe' external-link anchor in accent-11.
Cards have subtle border (gray-6), 1px, radius 8px, padding 20px. White-on-dark — use gray-2 bg.

## Section 2: Trend chart
A full-width card titled 'Revisions generated, last 30 days'. In the top-right of the card: a segmented metric toggle ('Revisions' / 'Projects'), and a small ghost dropdown 'Last 30 days' (non-functional).
The chart itself: a line chart (use recharts or just custom SVG) with the X-axis as dates (last 30 days) and the Y-axis as daily revision count. A single solid line in accent-9 with a subtle gradient fill underneath (accent-9 at 20% fading to 0%). Use mock values that ramp up from ~80/day at the start to ~220/day at the end (shows an escalating usage pattern that explains why they're near limit). Faint horizontal dotted line at y = 'daily burn rate to hit limit' (~166/day) labeled 'Pace to cap' in small muted text on the right. Chart height 240px. No grid lines except horizontal at every 50.

## Section 3: Top users table
Full-width card titled 'Top users this period' with a subtitle 'Ranked by revisions generated'.
A clean table (not AG-Grid — just a plain table with Radix styling) with these columns: #, User, Revisions, % of org, Projects, Last active.
Rows for Stark Industries (3 rows):
  1. tony@stark.com — 2,171 revisions — 47% — 82 projects — 12 min ago
  2. pepper@stark.com — 1,340 revisions — 29% — 61 projects — 2 hours ago
  3. happy@stark.com — 647 revisions — 14% — 31 projects — yesterday
  (There's also a 'rhodey@stark.com — 462 revisions — 10% — 12 projects — 4 days ago' row at the bottom for a total of 4 rows, even though 'Active users' says 3; the 4th is lower-activity.)
The 'Revisions' column has an inline horizontal micro-bar behind each number (same width math — tony's bar is full, others scaled). Use accent-9 at 30% alpha.
The '% of org' column uses a small pill-shaped badge showing the percentage in muted accent.
Row height ~44px, hover state gray-3, no row click behavior in this prototype.

## Section 4: Subscriptions
Full-width card titled 'Subscriptions'. Inside, a compact list (not a grid) of each subscription the org has. For Stark Industries, 1 subscription:
- Left: 'Pro plan' (bold) and 'sub_1Abc...' (muted small, monospace). A green Badge 'active'.
- Middle: 'Current period: Nov 1 → Nov 30, 2026'.
- Right: tiny Stripe link 'Open in Stripe' with an ExternalLink icon.
Just one row for the prototype, but style for multiples.

## Section 5: Quota status
Full-width card titled 'Quotas'. A table with columns Metric, Used, Soft limit, Hard limit, Status.
Two rows:
- Revisions generated — 4,620 — 4,000 — 5,000 — status pill 'Approaching hard limit' (orange, bold). An inline horizontal thin progress bar on the Used cell showing 92%.
- Projects created — 186 — 500 — 1,000 — status pill 'Healthy' (green). Progress bar ~18%.

## Data/navigation behavior for the prototype
- On mount, the page shows Stark Industries' data (hard-code this as the 'default view' when you click the first Account row). If the user clicks other account rows, they see different stock data for those orgs — at least have Initech Solutions (3,180/5,000, 4 users), Wayne Enterprises (2,850/5,000, 3 users), Acme Corp (1,420/5,000, 3 users), Globex (890/5,000, 2 users, Free plan), and Umbrella (210/5,000, 1 user, Free plan) mapped. If you don't want to go that deep, make every account click show Stark's data with the org name swapped in the header — that's fine for a prototype.
- Back button returns to the Accounts grid view.
- Everything else in the app (other tabs) unaffected.

## Visual language
- Dark Radix theme (already set). Use gray-2 for card backgrounds, gray-6 for borders, accent-9 for primary chart/bars, orange-9 for warning states, green-9 for positive.
- Type scale: H2 for org name and section titles, regular body for table content, small muted for labels and timestamps.
- All cards have 1px gray-6 border, 8px radius.
- Keep the existing Tabs bar visible above — don't hide chrome. The detail page is in the 'Accounts' tab content area.

This is the 'data-dense, organized, familiar' bet on what an org detail page looks like. It prioritizes parallel access to all the facets (usage / trend / users / subscriptions / quotas) as equal citizens laid out in sections, rather than centering one of them."}
EOF

curl -sF 'file=@-;filename=spec-3-2.json' https://drive.orianna.ai/api/v2/upload <<'EOF'
{"spec": "Add a dedicated organization detail page where the chart is the hero — everything else is contextual supporting information. Clicking any Account row in the Accounts tab opens this page for that org (instead of the current edit dialog).

## Entry / navigation
- In the Accounts tab, row clicks swap the tab's content from the AG-Grid to the detail view. Keep the tab bar at the top visible.
- The detail view has a compact header strip:
  - Back button (small ghost with LeftArrow icon + 'All accounts') on far left.
  - Org name H2 center-left.
  - On the right: a tiny Badge ('Pro', accent), the MRR text '$249/mo', and a ghost 'Edit account' button.
This header is intentionally thin (~64px) — the chart needs room.

## Section 1: Hero chart (the whole point)
Full-width, ~520px tall. Titled 'Revisions generated · this billing period'. In the top-right: a segmented metric toggle ('Revisions' / 'Projects'), and a period selector dropdown 'Nov 1 – Nov 30 (current)'.

This is a stacked AREA chart where EACH USER IS A LAYER. Not a line per user; a stacked area so the chart's total height at any X-value is the org's total, and the colored bands show who contributed how much on each day.

- X-axis: daily, Nov 1 → Nov 30.
- Y-axis: cumulative daily revisions.
- Bands (bottom to top, largest contributor at the bottom per convention):
  - tony@stark.com — 2,171 total, about 70/day ramping to 120/day. Color accent-9.
  - pepper@stark.com — 1,340 total, about 35/day ramping to 75/day. Color accent-7.
  - happy@stark.com — 647 total, fairly flat ~20/day. Color accent-5.
  - rhodey@stark.com — 462 total, some spikes on 3 specific days (zero on most days). Color accent-3.
- A dashed horizontal threshold line at y = soft_limit (4,000 cumulative, or mapped to a daily rate). Labeled 'Soft limit (4,000)'. A dashed red line at y = 5,000 labeled 'Hard limit'.
- A thin CUMULATIVE LINE overlay on top (darker, same accent) showing total cumulative revisions reaching 4,620 on day 30. This line is what shows we're at 92% of the hard limit.
- Legend: a horizontal legend strip just above the chart — each entry is an avatar circle (colored) + email + current total revisions + %. Clicking one isolates that band (non-functional in prototype, but render a subtle hover highlight on the legend entry).
- Tooltip on hover: shows the date, each user's contribution that day, and the running total. This would be nice — build it if tractable; otherwise just a static 'Nov 15: 2,840 total' floating label pinned on the mid-point of the chart as a stand-in.
- Background of the chart: a faint vertical accent gradient under the dashed soft-limit line (subtle orange tint) to visually call out 'in danger zone' region.

## Section 2: Supporting strip (compact, below the chart)
A single horizontal strip, ~120px tall, split into 3 regions divided by thin vertical gray-6 borders:

### Left third: Current period summary
- Label row 'This period' in gray-11 small.
- Big number '4,620 / 5,000 revisions' with the '4,620' in H2 accent color, '/ 5,000' muted.
- Subtitle 'Resets in 9 days · 92% of hard limit' with tiny orange AlertTriangle icon.

### Middle third: Top contributor
- Label 'Top contributor'. Beneath: a row with an avatar circle (colored accent-9 w/ initials 'TS'), the email 'tony@stark.com' bold, then '2,171 revisions (47%)' below. Under that: a small pill 'Increasing · +38% vs last period' in orange.

### Right third: Concentration
- Label 'Concentration'. Beneath: a horizontal mini-stacked bar (like a donut but flattened) showing the user split: tony 47%, pepper 29%, happy 14%, rhodey 10%. Color-matched to the chart bands. Below the bar: small text '47% of usage from 1 user' in H4 bold.

## Section 3: Quick context drawer (collapsed by default, at the bottom)
A single ghost 'Show account details' toggle row at the bottom, underlined. When expanded (default expanded for this prototype so the info is visible), it reveals a grid of 2x2 small info cards:
  - 'Plan': Pro · $249/mo. (Tiny 'Manage in Stripe' link.)
  - 'Members': 3 members · stark.com, starkindustries.com.
  - 'Owner': pepper@stark.com.
  - 'Quotas': Revisions 92% · Projects 18%. Tiny 2-bar mini-visual.
The drawer is intentionally secondary — the chart is the hero, these details are supporting.

## Data/navigation behavior
- Default state: Stark Industries. If the user clicks a different account, ideally swap the headline and data (ok if all orgs show Stark's data with just the org name swapped — it's a prototype).
- Back button returns to Accounts grid.
- The chart should render on load; the user shouldn't need to interact to see the key insight.

## Visual language
- Dark Radix theme. Chart area should pop visually — use deep, saturated accent colors for the bands so the user layers are distinguishable.
- Keep the overall page feeling very chart-forward. The chart should be the first thing someone's eye lands on and should answer 'who's using what' in seconds.
- No AG-Grid on this page — use plain elements for the supporting strip.

This is the 'chart is the answer, everything else is supporting' bet on what an org detail page looks like. It insists that a time-series with per-user banding tells the story faster than any table of numbers."}
EOF

curl -sF 'file=@-;filename=spec-3-3.json' https://drive.orianna.ai/api/v2/upload <<'EOF'
{"spec": "Add a dedicated organization detail page laid out as a two-column split: the left half is about the organization's usage, the right half is about the people inside the organization. Clicking any Account row in the Accounts tab opens this page for that org (instead of the current edit dialog).

## Entry / navigation
- Clicking an Account row swaps the tab's content from the AG-Grid to the detail view.
- The header strip above the split:
  - Back button (ghost, LeftArrow + 'All accounts') on far left.
  - Org name H2 with a tiny Badge ('Pro') beside it. Below, a muted subtitle line: 'stark.com · Created Jan 14, 2026 · Owner pepper@stark.com'.
  - Right side: ghost 'Edit account' button (opens existing AccountEditor dialog).

## Main body: 50/50 split with a thin vertical gray-6 divider
Use CSS grid: two equal columns, gap=0 (the divider does the separation). Each column scrolls independently. Columns have 32px internal padding.

### LEFT COLUMN: 'Organization usage'
A column title 'Organization usage' at the top in H3, with a tiny muted period label beside it 'Nov 1 – Nov 30 (current)'.

#### Sub-section 1: Headline usage card
A hero card at the top. Contents:
- Big number on the left: '4,620 / 5,000' — H1, 48px, bold. '4,620' in accent-11, '/ 5,000' muted.
- Below the number: small subtitle 'Revisions generated · 92% of hard limit · resets in 9 days' with a tiny orange AlertTriangle icon.
- Below that: a fat horizontal progress bar (height 12px) with the fill in orange-9 (gradient from orange-8 to orange-10). The bar has two ticks marked on it — a dashed line at 80% labeled 'Soft limit' and the right edge is the hard limit. The fill extends to 92%.

#### Sub-section 2: 30-day trend chart
A card titled 'Trend' with a metric toggle ('Revisions' / 'Projects') in the top-right. Inside: a simple line chart, height ~200px, showing daily revisions for the last 30 days. Single line in accent-9. No per-user banding here (that's in the right column). A horizontal dashed reference line at ~166/day labeled 'Pace to hard limit'. The line in the chart crosses above this threshold around day 20 — showing they've been on a burn pace.

#### Sub-section 3: Quotas
A card titled 'Quotas'. Inside, a compact table-style list (not AG-Grid):
Row per metric, each row is:
- Left: metric name ('Revisions generated', 'Projects created').
- Middle: a horizontal progress bar with 'X / Y' label overlaid in the center of the bar.
- Right: status pill ('Near limit' orange, 'Healthy' green).

#### Sub-section 4: Subscriptions
A card titled 'Subscriptions'. Compact rows, each row has:
- Plan name bold + 'active' Badge (green).
- Period dates ('Nov 1 → Nov 30').
- MRR ('$249/mo').
- Tiny 'Open in Stripe' link with ExternalLink icon.
Just one row for Stark but style for multiples.

### RIGHT COLUMN: 'People'
A column title 'People' at the top in H3, with a muted count beside it '3 active · 1 inactive (last 30 days)'.

#### Sub-section 1: Members summary card
A compact card at the top with two small stats side by side:
- 'Total members': 3 (with +1 ghost 'Invite' button)
- 'Active this period': 3
Below the stats: a very subtle horizontal face-pile of all members — colored avatar circles with initials (TS, PP, HH, RH), slightly overlapping. Hover shows tooltip with email.

#### Sub-section 2: Ranked member list (the meat of this column)
A card titled 'Members · ranked by revisions this period' with a small segmented sort toggle in the top-right ('Revisions' default / 'Projects' / 'Last active').

Each member is a rich row, ~80px tall. Rows have a hover state (gray-3). Row contents (left-to-right):
- Rank badge: circle '#1' in accent. Gray for others.
- Avatar circle: 40px, colored, initials.
- Identity stack:
  - Email bold (e.g. 'tony@stark.com').
  - Tiny muted line: 'Last active 12 min ago'.
- Stats (2 columns, right-aligned):
  - Revisions: bold number '2,171', below muted '47% of org'. A horizontal mini-bar behind the number showing % of org total (so tony's is nearly full, happy's is ~30%).
  - Projects: bold number '82', below muted '+12 this week'.
- Trailing small icon-button '•••' (Ellipsis) for row menu (non-functional).

Rows for Stark Industries (rank order):
  1. tony@stark.com — 2,171 revs · 47% — 82 projects · +12 this week — Last active 12 min ago
  2. pepper@stark.com — 1,340 revs · 29% — 61 projects · +8 this week — Last active 2 hours ago
  3. happy@stark.com — 647 revs · 14% — 31 projects · +2 this week — Last active yesterday
  4. rhodey@stark.com — 462 revs · 10% — 12 projects · 0 this week — Last active 4 days ago (muted — inactive)

4th row has slightly reduced opacity (~70%) to indicate inactivity, and the 'Last active' text is in red-11.

#### Sub-section 3: Usage share pie
A small card titled 'Usage share' containing a simple donut chart (~180px diameter) showing each member's share of revisions. Segments color-match the member avatars. Center of donut: total '4,620' with 'revisions' below in small muted. Legend to the right with color swatch + email + %.

## Data/navigation behavior
- Default: Stark Industries. Other account clicks either load that org's data or reuse Stark's data with name swap (prototype-level fidelity is fine).
- Back returns to Accounts grid.

## Visual language
- Dark Radix theme. The vertical divider between columns uses gray-6.
- Accent color is the primary signal for positive/neutral info; orange for approaching-limit; green for healthy; red for attention.
- Member rows should feel human — avatars, relative timestamps, last-active status matters. This column is where a PM learns 'who'.
- The left column is clinical/quantitative. The right is human/qualitative (ish).
- No AG-Grid on this page — plain CSS/Radix components throughout.

This is the 'the question is two questions, split them' bet. It says: an org detail page has to answer both 'what's the usage picture' and 'who's behind it' with equal prominence — putting them side-by-side makes the comparison natural."}
EOF

1	---
2	name: generate-prototype
3	description: "Build a standalone prototype app from the baseline clone that implements a design idea, run it on its own port, and register it on the canvas."
4	skills:
5	  - softlight:start-tunnel
6	model: opus
7	effort: medium
8	---
9	
10	# Generate Prototype
11	
12	You are building a standalone prototype — a copy of the baseline app with design changes made
13	directly in the source code. Each prototype is its own running application with its own tunnel,
14	not a script injected into a shared app.
15	
16	## Input
17	
18	### `<project_id>`
19	
20	Softlight project UUID.
21	
22	### `<slot_id>`
23	
24	Canvas slot UUID.
25	
26	### `<spec_url>`
27	
28	Drive URL of the design spec. Download it with `curl` first. The response is JSON — extract the `spec` field. That text is a description of the desired change to the application. May contain image URLs (design mocks, screenshots of the current prototype or baseline) — examine these for additional visual context. Use `curl` to download each image URL to a local file first, then
29	**Read** it. Do **not** use WebFetch for images — it returns binary data as text and cannot
30	render them.
31	
32	### `<images>`
33	
34	Optional. Image URLs or local file paths, one per line. If these are passed, view all of these before writing code. The spec references them.
35	
36	### `<caption_slot_id>`
37	
38	Optional. Canvas slot UUID for the caption below this prototype. If provided, fill it in after
39	registering the prototype (see Phase 6).
40	
41	### `<baseline_dir>`
42	
43	The directory path of the baseline clone — the already-built, runnable app created by
44	`clone-app-codegen`. This is your starting point.
45	
46	### `<prototype_dir>`
47	
48	Optional. If this prototype is a revision of an existing one, the directory path of the
49	previous prototype app. Start from there instead of copying the baseline fresh — it already
50	has the prior design changes applied.
51	
52	### `<context>`
53	
54	Optional pre-explored source code and analysis from the caller. May cover routing, auth, data
55	fetching, response shapes, and styling patterns.
56	
57	**Explore the codebase yourself.** You have full access to the source code — use it to understand
58	whatever you need about the app. Read local source files, not the tunnel URL.
59	
60	## Phase 1: Create the prototype app
61	
62	1. **Copy the source.** If `<prototype_dir>` was provided (revising an existing prototype),
63	   copy that directory — it already has the prior design changes. Otherwise, copy the baseline:
64	   ```bash
65	   cp -r <prototype_dir or baseline_dir> /tmp/prototype_<slot_id>
66	   ```
67	   Install dependencies with `pnpm` (fast, deduplicates across prototypes automatically):
68	   ```bash
69	   cd /tmp/prototype_<slot_id> && pnpm install
70	   ```
71	
72	2. **Read the spec.** Download `<spec_url>` with `curl`, extract the `spec` field. View any
73	   referenced images. Understand what design change is being requested.
74	
75	3. **Explore the codebase.** Read the baseline clone's files and the original application
76	   source to understand the component structure, design system, routing, data shapes, and
77	   styling. You need deep understanding to make changes that feel native to the app.
78	
79	4. **Make the design changes.** Edit the prototype's files to implement the spec. The result
80	   should feel like a well-designed, fully functioning app — not a rough mockup. If your
81	   changes introduce new UI that needs data, seed realistic mock data.
82	
83	   ### Styling rules
84	
85	   **Use inline styles for layout on new elements.** When you create new containers or
86	   wrappers, put layout properties — `display`, `flexDirection`, `alignItems`,
87	   `justifyContent`, `gap`, `padding`, `width`, `maxWidth`, `gridTemplateColumns` — directly
88	   on the element as inline styles. This keeps layout visible and co-located with the JSX,
89	   preventing cascade bugs from a separate CSS file. Do not create new CSS classes for layout.
90	
91	   **Use the app's existing CSS classes for visual styling.** Colors, fonts, borders, shadows,
92	   border-radius, transitions — these come from the app's design system. Read the existing CSS
93	   to find the right classes and values. Never hardcode approximate colors. Never load external
94	   CSS frameworks or component libraries when the app already has its own.
95	
96	   **Do not modify existing CSS class definitions.** If you need different behavior, create a
97	   new element with inline styles rather than changing a class that other elements depend on.
98	
99	5. **Build and serve the app.** Run a production build, then serve it on a free port.
100	   **Do NOT use the default port (5173)** — multiple prototypes run in parallel and will
101	   collide. Find a free port and bind to `127.0.0.1` explicitly (avoid IPv6 `[::1]`
102	   ambiguity):
103	   ```bash
104	   cd /tmp/prototype_<slot_id> && pnpm build
105	   ```
106	   Fix any build errors until the build succeeds, then serve the production build:
107	   ```bash
108	   PORT=$(python3 -c "import socket; s=socket.socket(); s.bind(('',0)); print(s.getsockname()[1]); s.close()")
109	   cd /tmp/prototype_<slot_id> && pnpm preview --host 127.0.0.1 --port $PORT --strictPort &
110	   ```
111	   Wait for the server to print the port it's listening on. Capture that port number.
112	
113	## Phase 2: Start a tunnel
114	
115	Run the `start-tunnel` skill with the port number from Phase 1. It returns a `tunnel_id` and
116	`PID`. Capture the `tunnel_id` — you'll need it for Phase 3, Phase 4, and Phase 5.
117	
118	## Phase 3: Validate in the browser
119	
120	After the build succeeds and `pnpm preview` is running, you MUST validate that the app renders
121	correctly with no runtime errors. A successful `pnpm build` does not guarantee the app works —
122	React components can crash at render time from missing data, bad hooks, or other issues that
123	only surface in the browser.
124	
125	Use the `plugin:softlight:playwright` MCP tools:
126	
127	1. Call `create_session` to get an isolated browser instance.
128	2. Call `browser_resize` to set the viewport to 1716x1065.
129	3. Call `browser_navigate` to `https://softlight.orianna.ai/api/tunnel/{tunnel_id}/`.
130	4. Call `browser_snapshot` and verify the page has real rendered content —
131	   not a blank white page, not a React error overlay, not a "Loading..."
132	   spinner stuck forever. If the snapshot shows a real rendered page with
133	   visible UI elements, the app is working.
134	
135	If the page is broken (blank, error overlay, nothing rendered):
136	1. Call `browser_console_messages` to diagnose. Look for fatal errors:
137	   uncaught exceptions, failed module loads, React error boundaries.
138	   Ignore non-fatal noise like deprecation warnings, third-party script
139	   errors, or React dev-mode warnings — most apps have these and they
140	   don't mean anything is broken.
141	2. Fix the source code in the prototype directory.
142	3. Rebuild with `pnpm build` and restart `pnpm preview --host`.
143	4. Re-check in the browser.
144	
145	When validation passes, call `close_session` to clean up the browser.
146	
147	## Phase 4: Screenshot the prototype
148	
149	You MUST use the `plugin:softlight:playwright` MCP for all browser interactions. All standard Playwright browser tools are available through this MCP. It is a thin wrapper around Playwright MCP that gives each session its own isolated browser, so multiple prototype agents can browse in parallel without conflicts.
150	
151	Open the prototype in a browser and screenshot it so reviewers can see the design changes. The task is to take screenshot(s) of the prototype in states where the design change(s) are visible.
152	
153	If the page isn't loading or the browser becomes unresponsive, check the preview server output for build errors, fix them, and retry. Try up to 3 times before giving up. If the prototype still won't load, return whatever screenshots you managed to capture (even none) and move on.
154	
155	Call `create_session` to get an isolated browser instance. Resize the viewport to 1716x1065.
156	Ensure you find the design change(s) so you can screenshot the design
157	changes and look at it. You may need to interact with the prototype to find all the design
158	changes to screenshot them (the codebase, spec_url, and source code can help you figure out what screenshots you need to take).
159	
160	1. Navigate to `https://softlight.orianna.ai/api/tunnel/{tunnel_id}/`
161	2. Check that the page loaded, then find the design changes described in the spec. You  may need to interact with the application to get the app into a state where the design change is visible. Reminder: pages could be broken or stuck loading. If that happens, move on — do not wait indefinitely.
162	3. Take a screenshot with `browser_take_screenshot` (`fullPage` set to `true`). This returns a URL.
163	4. Call `close_session` to clean up the browser
164	
165	## Phase 5: Register on the canvas
166	
167	Call the `update_iframe_element` MCP tool with `project_id`, `slot_id`, `tunnel_id` (the new
168	tunnel from Phase 2), `spec_url` (the `<spec_url>` from your input — pass it through
169	unchanged), `screenshot_urls` (the **drive URLs** from Phase 4), and `preview_url` set to the
170	screenshot that best represents the core of the design change.
171	
172	## Phase 6: Fill in the caption
173	
174	If `<caption_slot_id>` was provided, call `update_text_element` with `project_id`,
175	`slot_id` set to the `<caption_slot_id>`, and a short `text` (1-2 sentences) describing what
176	this prototype does, how it solves the problem, and what its tradeoffs are.
177

---
name: generate-prototype
description: "Build a standalone prototype app from the baseline clone that implements a design idea, run it on its own port, and register it on the canvas."
skills:
  - softlight:start-tunnel
model: opus
effort: medium
---

# Generate Prototype

You are building a standalone prototype — a copy of the baseline app with design changes made
directly in the source code. Each prototype is its own running application with its own tunnel,
not a script injected into a shared app.

## Input

### `<project_id>`

Softlight project UUID.

### `<slot_id>`

Canvas slot UUID.

### `<spec_url>`

Drive URL of the design spec. Download it with `curl` first. The response is JSON — extract the `spec` field. That text is a description of the desired change to the application. May contain image URLs (design mocks, screenshots of the current prototype or baseline) — examine these for additional visual context. Use `curl` to download each image URL to a local file first, then
**Read** it. Do **not** use WebFetch for images — it returns binary data as text and cannot
render them.

### `<images>`

Optional. Image URLs or local file paths, one per line. If these are passed, view all of these before writing code. The spec references them.

### `<caption_slot_id>`

Optional. Canvas slot UUID for the caption below this prototype. If provided, fill it in after
registering the prototype (see Phase 6).

### `<baseline_dir>`

The directory path of the baseline clone — the already-built, runnable app created by
`clone-app-codegen`. This is your starting point.

### `<prototype_dir>`

Optional. If this prototype is a revision of an existing one, the directory path of the
previous prototype app. Start from there instead of copying the baseline fresh — it already
has the prior design changes applied.

### `<context>`

Optional pre-explored source code and analysis from the caller. May cover routing, auth, data
fetching, response shapes, and styling patterns.

**Explore the codebase yourself.** You have full access to the source code — use it to understand
whatever you need about the app. Read local source files, not the tunnel URL.

## Phase 1: Create the prototype app

1. **Copy the source.** If `<prototype_dir>` was provided (revising an existing prototype),
   copy that directory — it already has the prior design changes. Otherwise, copy the baseline:
   ```bash
   cp -r <prototype_dir or baseline_dir> /tmp/prototype_<slot_id>
   ```
   Install dependencies with `pnpm` (fast, deduplicates across prototypes automatically):
   ```bash
   cd /tmp/prototype_<slot_id> && pnpm install
   ```

2. **Read the spec.** Download `<spec_url>` with `curl`, extract the `spec` field. View any
   referenced images. Understand what design change is being requested.

3. **Explore the codebase.** Read the baseline clone's files and the original application
   source to understand the component structure, design system, routing, data shapes, and
   styling. You need deep understanding to make changes that feel native to the app.

4. **Make the design changes.** Edit the prototype's files to implement the spec. The result
   should feel like a well-designed, fully functioning app — not a rough mockup. If your
   changes introduce new UI that needs data, seed realistic mock data.

   ### Styling rules

   **Use inline styles for layout on new elements.** When you create new containers or
   wrappers, put layout properties — `display`, `flexDirection`, `alignItems`,
   `justifyContent`, `gap`, `padding`, `width`, `maxWidth`, `gridTemplateColumns` — directly
   on the element as inline styles. This keeps layout visible and co-located with the JSX,
   preventing cascade bugs from a separate CSS file. Do not create new CSS classes for layout.

   **Use the app's existing CSS classes for visual styling.** Colors, fonts, borders, shadows,
   border-radius, transitions — these come from the app's design system. Read the existing CSS
   to find the right classes and values. Never hardcode approximate colors. Never load external
   CSS frameworks or component libraries when the app already has its own.

   **Do not modify existing CSS class definitions.** If you need different behavior, create a
   new element with inline styles rather than changing a class that other elements depend on.

5. **Build and serve the app.** Run a production build, then serve it on a free port.
   **Do NOT use the default port (5173)** — multiple prototypes run in parallel and will
   collide. Find a free port and bind to `127.0.0.1` explicitly (avoid IPv6 `[::1]`
   ambiguity):
   ```bash
   cd /tmp/prototype_<slot_id> && pnpm build
   ```
   Fix any build errors until the build succeeds, then serve the production build:
   ```bash
   PORT=$(python3 -c "import socket; s=socket.socket(); s.bind(('',0)); print(s.getsockname()[1]); s.close()")
   cd /tmp/prototype_<slot_id> && pnpm preview --host 127.0.0.1 --port $PORT --strictPort &
   ```
   Wait for the server to print the port it's listening on. Capture that port number.

## Phase 2: Start a tunnel

Run the `start-tunnel` skill with the port number from Phase 1. It returns a `tunnel_id` and
`PID`. Capture the `tunnel_id` — you'll need it for Phase 3, Phase 4, and Phase 5.

## Phase 3: Validate in the browser

After the build succeeds and `pnpm preview` is running, you MUST validate that the app renders
correctly with no runtime errors. A successful `pnpm build` does not guarantee the app works —
React components can crash at render time from missing data, bad hooks, or other issues that
only surface in the browser.

Use the `plugin:softlight:playwright` MCP tools:

1. Call `create_session` to get an isolated browser instance.
2. Call `browser_resize` to set the viewport to 1716x1065.
3. Call `browser_navigate` to `https://softlight.orianna.ai/api/tunnel/{tunnel_id}/`.
4. Call `browser_snapshot` and verify the page has real rendered content —
   not a blank white page, not a React error overlay, not a "Loading..."
   spinner stuck forever. If the snapshot shows a real rendered page with
   visible UI elements, the app is working.

If the page is broken (blank, error overlay, nothing rendered):
1. Call `browser_console_messages` to diagnose. Look for fatal errors:
   uncaught exceptions, failed module loads, React error boundaries.
   Ignore non-fatal noise like deprecation warnings, third-party script
   errors, or React dev-mode warnings — most apps have these and they
   don't mean anything is broken.
2. Fix the source code in the prototype directory.
3. Rebuild with `pnpm build` and restart `pnpm preview --host`.
4. Re-check in the browser.

When validation passes, call `close_session` to clean up the browser.

## Phase 4: Screenshot the prototype

You MUST use the `plugin:softlight:playwright` MCP for all browser interactions. All standard Playwright browser tools are available through this MCP. It is a thin wrapper around Playwright MCP that gives each session its own isolated browser, so multiple prototype agents can browse in parallel without conflicts.

Open the prototype in a browser and screenshot it so reviewers can see the design changes. The task is to take screenshot(s) of the prototype in states where the design change(s) are visible.

If the page isn't loading or the browser becomes unresponsive, check the preview server output for build errors, fix them, and retry. Try up to 3 times before giving up. If the prototype still won't load, return whatever screenshots you managed to capture (even none) and move on.

Call `create_session` to get an isolated browser instance. Resize the viewport to 1716x1065.
Ensure you find the design change(s) so you can screenshot the design
changes and look at it. You may need to interact with the prototype to find all the design
changes to screenshot them (the codebase, spec_url, and source code can help you figure out what screenshots you need to take).

1. Navigate to `https://softlight.orianna.ai/api/tunnel/{tunnel_id}/`
2. Check that the page loaded, then find the design changes described in the spec. You  may need to interact with the application to get the app into a state where the design change is visible. Reminder: pages could be broken or stuck loading. If that happens, move on — do not wait indefinitely.
3. Take a screenshot with `browser_take_screenshot` (`fullPage` set to `true`). This returns a URL.
4. Call `close_session` to clean up the browser

## Phase 5: Register on the canvas

Call the `update_iframe_element` MCP tool with `project_id`, `slot_id`, `tunnel_id` (the new
tunnel from Phase 2), `spec_url` (the `<spec_url>` from your input — pass it through
unchanged), `screenshot_urls` (the **drive URLs** from Phase 4), and `preview_url` set to the
screenshot that best represents the core of the design change.

## Phase 6: Fill in the caption

If `<caption_slot_id>` was provided, call `update_text_element` with `project_id`,
`slot_id` set to the `<caption_slot_id>`, and a short `text` (1-2 sentences) describing what
this prototype does, how it solves the problem, and what its tradeoffs are.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: present-canvas
3	description: "Own the canvas as a communication artifact. Organize and narrate the designer's exploration so the PM can review it and make decisions."
4	model: opus
5	effort: medium
6	---
7	
8	You are the creative director of a design canvas. A product designer has explored a hard product and
9	design problem — analyzing tensions, creating prototypes, going deep. Your job is to present
10	that exploration so the PM can see the full landscape, understand the tradeoffs, and have
11	enough to make decisions.
12	
13	The designer creates explorations and kicks off prototypes. You handle everything the
14	human reads: narrative text that frames the work, spatial organization that makes the canvas
15	scannable, and clear signposting of where the PM's input matters most.
16	
17	You do three things:
18	1. **Write narrative text** — the connective tissue that turns a pile of explorations into a
19	   story. This isn't a recap of the designer's process — it's a senior designer's writeup
20	   for someone who needs to make calls. What it covers and in what order is determined by
21	   what the work actually is.
22	2. **Organize the canvas spatially** — arrange content into sections that tell a clear story,
23	   reposition explorations the designer created if they need better placement
24	3. **Make it reviewable** — the PM should finish reading and know what decisions are in front
25	   of them, what each direction trades off, and where their input matters most
26	
27	## Inputs
28	
29	- **`<project_id>`** — Softlight project UUID
30	- **`<mode>`** — `initial` (default) or `revision`. In revision mode, only position new slots
31	  and frame narrative as a response to PM feedback rather than a fresh problem framing.
32	- **`<thinking>`** — The designer's raw analysis, observations, decisions, or synthesis. In
33	  revision mode, this is the designer's analysis of the PM's feedback: what comments said,
34	  what they decided to explore in response, how this builds on previous work.
35	- **`<explorations_created>`** — What the designer just created: exploration titles, slot_ids,
36	  what each one explores and why. In revision mode, these are the ONLY new slots to position.
37	
38	## How you think about the canvas
39	
40	### Sections
41	
42	A section is where you go to war with a hard problem. It's not a tidy structure of "header,
43	analysis, exploration, done." It's the full messy arc of wrestling with something that resists
44	easy answers: the initial framing, the first round of exploration, the discovery that changes
45	the framing, the deeper exploration, the iteration, the counter-argument, the resolution or
46	the decision to live with a tradeoff.
47	
48	A section that's doing its job has:
49	- A clear statement of the problem — specific, opinionated, not generic.
50	- Evidence of genuine wrestling — not just one pass, but iteration, refinement, going deeper
51	
52	A section that's NOT doing its job:
53	- Frames the problem generically ("how should we handle X?")
54	- Doesn't go deep enough that a stakeholder would trust the conclusion
55	
56	A section isn't defined by the level of abstraction — direction, idea, sub-idea, visual. Hard
57	problems exist at every level. Sometimes it's "what strategic direction?" Sometimes it's "how
58	should this one interaction work?" Either can warrant deep, sustained work.
59	
60	There is no formal "section" object — you create sections by placing related content near each
61	other and leaving distance from unrelated content. Where sections go should reflect the
62	structure of the problem: independent problems sit side by side, deep dives go below or to
63	the right of what they branched from.
64	
65	## You own layout
66	
67	**In `initial` mode:** You are the **sole authority** on where things go on the canvas. The
68	designer creates explorations and prototypes — they start at approximate positions (auto-placed
69	below existing content), but these are rough placements. It's your job to read the canvas,
70	figure out what exists, and arrange everything into a coherent layout. No one else decides the
71	final positioning.
72	
73	**In `revision` mode:** The canvas already has content from previous rounds — explorations,
74	narrative text, comment threads. That existing content is settled. New content goes **below the
75	most recent revision**. Find the bottom edge of existing content and build down from there.
76	You are the authority on how new content is laid out from that point — how explorations are
77	arranged relative to each other, where narrative text goes, how sections are structured. But
78	**do NOT move existing slots** — only reposition the slots listed in `<explorations_created>`.
79	
80	## What you do
81	
82	1. **Read the canvas first.** Call `get_project` to see every slot — its id, type, position,
83	   and dimensions. You need this to avoid overlaps and to understand the current layout. Every
84	   slot has `x`, `y`, `width`, and `height` fields. Read them all before placing or moving
85	   anything.
86	
87	2. **Plan the layout.** Based on what exists, decide where everything should go. Narrative
88	   text goes above explorations. Explorations within a section sit side by side or stacked.
89	   Sections are separated by vertical space. Think through the full layout before making any
90	   tool calls — including how much text you'll write and how tall it will be (use the
91	   reference dimensions below). In revision mode, find the bottom edge of existing content
92	   and plan new content below it.
93	
94	3. **Refine positions with `move_slots` — do this BEFORE creating any text.**
95	   The designer's explorations start at rough auto-placed positions (stacked below existing content). In initial mode, reposition every slot. In revision mode, only reposition the slots from `<explorations_created>` — do not move existing slots. Move titles, prototypes, and captions into the layout you've planned. Each exploration consists of a title slot, N prototype slots in a row, and N caption slots below them. Move them all as a group, preserving their relative spacing (title at top, prototypes
96	   160 units below title, each prototype 1840 apart horizontally, captions 1160 below
97	   prototypes). Complete ALL positioning before creating any text — this ensures
98	   explorations are in their final positions before narrative appears, so the canvas never
99	   looks broken mid-update. You have `move_slots` for moving multiple slots in one batch.
100	
101	4. **Then write narrative text with `create_text_element`.** Place each element at specific x,y
102	   coordinates that don't overlap with anything. Choose the right typographic variant:
103	   - `h1` — the problem being solved, major section headers
104	   - `h2` — key questions, section headers for sub-problems
105	   - `h3` — aspects of a problem, sub-questions
106	   - `p` — analysis, reasoning, observations, transitions
107	   - `small` — notes, caveats
108	
109	   **Never pass `width`.** All text uses the default 1720, even above a wider exploration —
110	   wider than 1720 is unreadable. The only exception is the pre-made title slot from
111	   `create_exploration`, which is fixed at the exploration's full width and can't be resized;
112	   keep its text to a short label (≤6 words). Anything longer goes in its own
113	   `create_text_element` block at 1720.
114	
115	5. **Build narrative that shows the arc of thinking.** The narrative isn't labeling — it's
116	   the connective tissue that makes the explorations make sense. Let the work determine the
117	   shape: a hard insight might be the thing that needs to land first; a tension might be
118	   the center of gravity; a single section might deserve long wrestling while a transition
119	   earns a line. Headers come from what the section is actually about, not from a fixed
120	   inventory of section types. The canvas should feel like it was written in response to
121	   this specific problem, not poured into a template. In revision mode, focus on what's new
122	   and why — don't re-establish full original context; connect the PM's feedback to the new
123	   work in whatever way actually helps the PM decide.
124	
125	6. **Make it clear where input is needed — by weaving it in, not labeling it.** The PM
126	   should know what to react to, but that cue belongs inside the narrative, not as a
127	   recurring "Where your input matters" header under every exploration. Tell them what the
128	   decision is and what you'd push them on, in the same voice you've been using. A ritual
129	   phrase repeated under every exploration reads as template, not thought.
130	
131	## How to write
132	
133	Write like a senior designer — direct, specific, opinionated.
134	
135	Name specific things. The specific screens, the specific friction points, the specific user
136	emotions. Generic observations are worthless.
137	
138	**Length varies with the wrestle.** A section where the designer is grappling with something
139	hard earns paragraphs; a setup section earns a sentence or two. Uniform section lengths read
140	as template-filling. The canvas is visual — text should frame the prototypes, not overwhelm
141	them — but don't flatten a genuine insight into the same shape as a transition.
142	
143	**Don't leak the scaffolding.** The designer reasons in framings and decision trees; you
144	don't write about those on the canvas. Never produce headers like "Framings we considered,"
145	"Framings we committed to," or "Framings we rejected." Avoid the word "framing" in
146	user-facing text entirely — say what the insight *is*, not that it was a framing. No section
147	should be meta-commentary about the designer's process. The PM should absorb the thinking
148	by reading insights, not by reading about how the designer thought.
149	
150	**No recurring ritual phrases.** If you find yourself writing the same header or opener
151	under each exploration ("Where your input matters here," "How these explorations relate"),
152	stop — you're filling a template. Each section should sound like its own thing, earning its
153	header from what it's actually about.
154	
155	## What you return
156	
157	After writing and organizing, return a brief summary of what you composed — what text you
158	wrote, where you placed it, any reorganization.
159	
160	## Reference dimensions
161	
162	Canvas units:
163	- Prototype: 1720 × 1120. Gap between prototypes: 120.
164	- Exploration of N prototypes: (N × 1840 − 120) wide, ~1520 tall.
165	- Text line heights: h1 ~135, h2 ~112, h3 ~90, p ~60, small ~42.
166	- Allow ~400-600 vertical units between sections.
167	- Allow ~200-400 vertical units between a text block and the exploration below it.

file.content:
---
name: present-canvas
description: "Own the canvas as a communication artifact. Organize and narrate the designer's exploration so the PM can review it and make decisions."
model: opus
effort: medium
---

You are the creative director of a design canvas. A product designer has explored a hard product and
design problem — analyzing tensions, creating prototypes, going deep. Your job is to present
that exploration so the PM can see the full landscape, understand the tradeoffs, and have
enough to make decisions.

The designer creates explorations and kicks off prototypes. You handle everything the
human reads: narrative text that frames the work, spatial organization that makes the canvas
scannable, and clear signposting of where the PM's input matters most.

You do three things:
1. **Write narrative text** — the connective tissue that turns a pile of explorations into a
   story. This isn't a recap of the designer's process — it's a senior designer's writeup
   for someone who needs to make calls. What it covers and in what order is determined by
   what the work actually is.
2. **Organize the canvas spatially** — arrange content into sections that tell a clear story,
   reposition explorations the designer created if they need better placement
3. **Make it reviewable** — the PM should finish reading and know what decisions are in front
   of them, what each direction trades off, and where their input matters most

## Inputs

- **`<project_id>`** — Softlight project UUID
- **`<mode>`** — `initial` (default) or `revision`. In revision mode, only position new slots
  and frame narrative as a response to PM feedback rather than a fresh problem framing.
- **`<thinking>`** — The designer's raw analysis, observations, decisions, or synthesis. In
  revision mode, this is the designer's analysis of the PM's feedback: what comments said,
  what they decided to explore in response, how this builds on previous work.
- **`<explorations_created>`** — What the designer just created: exploration titles, slot_ids,
  what each one explores and why. In revision mode, these are the ONLY new slots to position.

## How you think about the canvas

### Sections

A section is where you go to war with a hard problem. It's not a tidy structure of "header,
analysis, exploration, done." It's the full messy arc of wrestling with something that resists
easy answers: the initial framing, the first round of exploration, the discovery that changes
the framing, the deeper exploration, the iteration, the counter-argument, the resolution or
the decision to live with a tradeoff.

A section that's doing its job has:
- A clear statement of the problem — specific, opinionated, not generic.
- Evidence of genuine wrestling — not just one pass, but iteration, refinement, going deeper

A section that's NOT doing its job:
- Frames the problem generically ("how should we handle X?")
- Doesn't go deep enough that a stakeholder would trust the conclusion

A section isn't defined by the level of abstraction — direction, idea, sub-idea, visual. Hard
problems exist at every level. Sometimes it's "what strategic direction?" Sometimes it's "how
should this one interaction work?" Either can warrant deep, sustained work.

There is no formal "section" object — you create sections by placing related content near each
other and leaving distance from unrelated content. Where sections go should reflect the
structure of the problem: independent problems sit side by side, deep dives go below or to
the right of what they branched from.

## You own layout

**In `initial` mode:** You are the **sole authority** on where things go on the canvas. The
designer creates explorations and prototypes — they start at approximate positions (auto-placed
below existing content), but these are rough placements. It's your job to read the canvas,
figure out what exists, and arrange everything into a coherent layout. No one else decides the
final positioning.

**In `revision` mode:** The canvas already has content from previous rounds — explorations,
narrative text, comment threads. That existing content is settled. New content goes **below the
most recent revision**. Find the bottom edge of existing content and build down from there.
You are the authority on how new content is laid out from that point — how explorations are
arranged relative to each other, where narrative text goes, how sections are structured. But
**do NOT move existing slots** — only reposition the slots listed in `<explorations_created>`.

## What you do

1. **Read the canvas first.** Call `get_project` to see every slot — its id, type, position,
   and dimensions. You need this to avoid overlaps and to understand the current layout. Every
   slot has `x`, `y`, `width`, and `height` fields. Read them all before placing or moving
   anything.

2. **Plan the layout.** Based on what exists, decide where everything should go. Narrative
   text goes above explorations. Explorations within a section sit side by side or stacked.
   Sections are separated by vertical space. Think through the full layout before making any
   tool calls — including how much text you'll write and how tall it will be (use the
   reference dimensions below). In revision mode, find the bottom edge of existing content
   and plan new content below it.

3. **Refine positions with `move_slots` — do this BEFORE creating any text.**
   The designer's explorations start at rough auto-placed positions (stacked below existing content). In initial mode, reposition every slot. In revision mode, only reposition the slots from `<explorations_created>` — do not move existing slots. Move titles, prototypes, and captions into the layout you've planned. Each exploration consists of a title slot, N prototype slots in a row, and N caption slots below them. Move them all as a group, preserving their relative spacing (title at top, prototypes
   160 units below title, each prototype 1840 apart horizontally, captions 1160 below
   prototypes). Complete ALL positioning before creating any text — this ensures
   explorations are in their final positions before narrative appears, so the canvas never
   looks broken mid-update. You have `move_slots` for moving multiple slots in one batch.

4. **Then write narrative text with `create_text_element`.** Place each element at specific x,y
   coordinates that don't overlap with anything. Choose the right typographic variant:
   - `h1` — the problem being solved, major section headers
   - `h2` — key questions, section headers for sub-problems
   - `h3` — aspects of a problem, sub-questions
   - `p` — analysis, reasoning, observations, transitions
   - `small` — notes, caveats

   **Never pass `width`.** All text uses the default 1720, even above a wider exploration —
   wider than 1720 is unreadable. The only exception is the pre-made title slot from
   `create_exploration`, which is fixed at the exploration's full width and can't be resized;
   keep its text to a short label (≤6 words). Anything longer goes in its own
   `create_text_element` block at 1720.

5. **Build narrative that shows the arc of thinking.** The narrative isn't labeling — it's
   the connective tissue that makes the explorations make sense. Let the work determine the
   shape: a hard insight might be the thing that needs to land first; a tension might be
   the center of gravity; a single section might deserve long wrestling while a transition
   earns a line. Headers come from what the section is actually about, not from a fixed
   inventory of section types. The canvas should feel like it was written in response to
   this specific problem, not poured into a template. In revision mode, focus on what's new
   and why — don't re-establish full original context; connect the PM's feedback to the new
   work in whatever way actually helps the PM decide.

6. **Make it clear where input is needed — by weaving it in, not labeling it.** The PM
   should know what to react to, but that cue belongs inside the narrative, not as a
   recurring "Where your input matters" header under every exploration. Tell them what the
   decision is and what you'd push them on, in the same voice you've been using. A ritual
   phrase repeated under every exploration reads as template, not thought.

## How to write

Write like a senior designer — direct, specific, opinionated.

Name specific things. The specific screens, the specific friction points, the specific user
emotions. Generic observations are worthless.

**Length varies with the wrestle.** A section where the designer is grappling with something
hard earns paragraphs; a setup section earns a sentence or two. Uniform section lengths read
as template-filling. The canvas is visual — text should frame the prototypes, not overwhelm
them — but don't flatten a genuine insight into the same shape as a transition.

**Don't leak the scaffolding.** The designer reasons in framings and decision trees; you
don't write about those on the canvas. Never produce headers like "Framings we considered,"
"Framings we committed to," or "Framings we rejected." Avoid the word "framing" in
user-facing text entirely — say what the insight *is*, not that it was a framing. No section
should be meta-commentary about the designer's process. The PM should absorb the thinking
by reading insights, not by reading about how the designer thought.

**No recurring ritual phrases.** If you find yourself writing the same header or opener
under each exploration ("Where your input matters here," "How these explorations relate"),
stop — you're filling a template. Each section should sound like its own thing, earning its
header from what it's actually about.

## What you return

After writing and organizing, return a brief summary of what you composed — what text you
wrote, where you placed it, any reorganization.

## Reference dimensions

Canvas units:
- Prototype: 1720 × 1120. Gap between prototypes: 120.
- Exploration of N prototypes: (N × 1840 − 120) wide, ~1520 tall.
- Text line heights: h1 ~135, h2 ~112, h3 ~90, p ~60, small ~42.
- Allow ~400-600 vertical units between sections.
- Allow ~200-400 vertical units between a text block and the exploration below it.
- Text is always 1720 wide — never pass `width`, never stretch to match a wide exploration.
- Pre-made title slots span the exploration width and can't be resized; keep their text to a
  short label (≤6 words).

input.content:
# Shared context for billing usage-visibility prototype agents

## The app

A billing admin console at `server/billing/ui`. Seven tabs (Accounts default, Subscriptions, Prices, Upgrades, Quotas, Usage Events, Usages), Radix UI Themes (dark mode), AG-Grid for tables. Tab state lives in URL query `?tab=<name>` via react-router. Data is fetched with @tanstack/react-query.

The baseline clone you start from is a standalone Vite app that uses mock data (no server).

- Query definitions: `src/queries.ts` — exports `accountsQueryOptions`, `subscriptionsQueryOptions`, `pricesQueryOptions`, `quotasQueryOptions`, `upgradesQueryOptions`, `usageEventsQueryOptions`, `usagesQueryOptions`.
- Mock data: `src/mockData.ts` — exports `mockAccounts`, `mockPrices`, `mockSubscriptions`, `mockQuotas`, `mockUpgrades`, `mockUsageEvents`, `mockUsages`.
- Types: `src/types.ts`.
- App shell: `src/App.tsx`. Each `Tabs.Content` is wrapped in `ErrorBoundary` + `Suspense`.
- Tables: `src/components/tables/` (`AccountTable.tsx`, `SubscriptionTable.tsx`, `PriceTable.tsx`, `UpgradeTable.tsx`, `QuotaTable.tsx`, `UsageEventTable.tsx`, `UsageTable.tsx`, `SkeletonTable.tsx`).
- Editors: `src/components/editors/` — dialog forms for CRUD.
- Cells: `src/components/cells/` — small renderers for cross-reference cells (e.g., PriceCell, AccountCell).
- Icons: `src/icons/` — curated subset: `AlertCircleIcon`, `CheckIcon`, `Loader2Icon`, `PlusIcon`, `RefreshCwIcon`, `SaveIcon`, `Trash2Icon`, `UploadIcon`, `XIcon`, plus `createIcon.tsx` helper (takes SVG path data). For new icons (ArrowUp, ArrowDown, ExternalLink, Ellipsis, ChevronRight, ChevronDown, LeftArrow/ArrowLeft, AlertTriangle, TrendingUp, Users, etc), use `createIcon.tsx` — you can copy path data from the repo at `/workspaces/orianna/infrastructure/ts/icons/`, which has 57 icons (ArrowUpIcon.tsx, ArrowDownIcon.tsx, ExternalLinkIcon.tsx, EllipsisIcon.tsx, ChevronRightIcon.tsx, ChevronDownIcon.tsx, ArrowLeftIcon.tsx, AlertTriangleIcon.tsx, TrendingUpIcon.tsx, UsersIcon.tsx, etc.).

## The theme

Radix dark theme is already configured in `App.tsx` (`<Theme appearance="dark" accentColor="iris" radius="medium">` or similar — confirm by reading). AG-Grid uses `themeQuartz.withPart(colorSchemeDark)` with custom CSS variables bound to Radix tokens (`var(--gray-2)`, `var(--gray-11)`, `var(--accent-9)`, etc.). Use Radix CSS variables for any new colors:
- Backgrounds: `var(--gray-1)` (app bg), `var(--gray-2)` (card bg), `var(--gray-3)` (hover).
- Borders: `var(--gray-6)` (strong), `var(--gray-5)` (subtle).
- Text: `var(--gray-12)` (primary), `var(--gray-11)` (secondary).
- Accent: `var(--accent-9)` (primary), `var(--accent-11)` (links).
- Status: `var(--orange-9)` (warning), `var(--green-9)` (success), `var(--red-9)` (danger).

Use Radix components (`Flex`, `Card`, `Button`, `Badge`, `Progress`, `Dialog`, `Tabs`, `IconButton`, `Tooltip`, `Separator`, `Text`, `Heading`) from `@radix-ui/themes`. They're already installed.

## The usage/user data you need to seed

Current `mockUsages` values are small stubs (3-521). Replace them. Current `mockUsageEvents` has ~15 rows — you'll need many more to cover trend charts.

**Consistent mock numbers across all 9 prototypes** (hard_limit = 5,000 revisions for every org's revisions quota; hard_limit = 1,000 for projects):

| Org | Revisions | Projects | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stark Industries (`acc-005`, `sub-005`) | 4,620 / 5,000 | 186 | Pro · near limit (92%) |
| Initech Solutions (`acc-003`, `sub-003`) | 3,180 / 5,000 | 132 | Pro · healthy (64%) |
| Wayne Enterprises (`acc-006`, `sub-006`) | 2,850 / 5,000 | 98 | Pro · healthy (57%) |
| Acme Corp (`acc-001`, `sub-001`) | 1,420 / 5,000 | 54 | Starter (28%) |
| Globex Industries (`acc-002`, `sub-002`) | 890 / 5,000 | 38 | Free (18%) |
| Umbrella Research (`acc-004`, `sub-004`) | 210 / 5,000 | 9 | Free (4%) |

**Per-user contributions (the important split):**

- Stark: tony@stark.com 2,171 (47%), pepper@stark.com 1,340 (29%), happy@stark.com 647 (14%), rhodey@stark.com 462 (10%). *Add rhodey as a 4th member.*
- Initech: bill@initech.com 1,420 (45%), peter@initech.com 820 (26%), samir@initech.com 620 (19%), michael@initech.com 320 (10%).
- Wayne: bruce@wayne.com 1,580 (55%), alfred@wayne.com 790 (28%), lucius@wayne.com 480 (17%). *Add lucius as a 3rd member.*
- Acme: alice@acme.com 770 (54%), bob@acme.com 430 (30%), carol@acme.com 220 (16%).
- Globex: homer@globex.com 510 (57%), marge@globex.com 380 (43%).
- Umbrella: wesker@umbrella.com 210 (100%), jill@umbrella.com 0.

**Last-active timestamps:**
- tony: 12 min ago; pepper: 2 hr ago; happy: yesterday; rhodey: 4 days ago.
- bill: 30 min ago; peter: 4 hr ago; samir: 1 day ago; michael: 3 days ago.
- bruce: 1 hr ago; alfred: 8 hr ago; lucius: 2 days ago.
- alice: 2 hr ago; bob: 1 day ago; carol: 3 days ago.
- homer: 4 hr ago; marge: 1 day ago.
- wesker: 6 days ago; jill: 14 days ago.

**Billing period for "current":** 2026-04-01 → 2026-05-01. "Today" for the purposes of the chart is 2026-04-21 (day 21 of 30).

**Daily trend profile:** For each org, generate ~30 days of daily totals that sum to the current usage on day 21 (with ~9 zero-ish days remaining in the period but projected forward). Stark should show an escalating pattern that crosses above a 166/day threshold ("pace to hit hard limit"). For per-user stacked charts (specs 1.3 and 3.2), split each day's total proportionally across users with some randomness so bands have texture. `rhodey@stark.com` should be near-zero most days with 3 spike days. `jill@umbrella.com` should be zero throughout.

## Shared rules for every prototype

1. **Don't break other tabs.** Only modify the tab(s) or pages your spec touches. Every other tab should still render its baseline AG-Grid.
2. **Preserve the existing app shell.** Don't remove `ErrorBoundary`/`Suspense` wrappers, tab navigation, or the Radix theme provider.
3. **Use inline styles for layout on new elements** (display, flex, grid, gap, padding, widths, template-columns). Use CSS variables for colors. Don't add a new CSS framework.
4. **Mock data you add must type-check** — either extend `types.ts` with new fields/types or create a new companion file (e.g., `src/usageDetail.ts`) and import it where needed. Don't break the existing `Usage`/`UsageEvent`/`Account` consumers.
5. **React Query pattern:** if you add a new query, follow the `queryOptions` pattern in `queries.ts` (simulate `await delay(300)` then return an array). Use `useSuspenseQuery` in components so the Suspense fallback works.
6. **Numbers must match the spec.** If the spec says "4,620 / 5,000" and "47%", do not ship 4,621 or 46%.
7. **Performance.** Build with `pnpm build`, then `pnpm preview` on a free port — don't use 5173.

## If softlight MCP tools are unavailable in your environment

The softlight MCP may or may not be loaded as tools (`update_iframe_element`, `update_text_element`, `get_project`). If they are not in your tool list, fall back to calling the HTTP MCP directly via `curl`:

```bash
# 1. Initialize — capture mcp-session-id from response header
curl -s -D /tmp/hdr.txt -X POST https://softlight.orianna.ai/mcp/ \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Accept: application/json, text/event-stream' \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize","params":{"protocolVersion":"2024-11-05","capabilities":{},"clientInfo":{"name":"a","version":"1"}}}' > /dev/null
SESSION=$(grep -i '^mcp-session-id' /tmp/hdr.txt | awk '{print $2}' | tr -d '\r')

# 2. Notify initialized
curl -s -X POST https://softlight.orianna.ai/mcp/ \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Accept: application/json, text/event-stream' \
  -H "mcp-session-id: $SESSION" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"notifications/initialized","params":{}}' > /dev/null

# 3. Call a tool (example: update_iframe_element)
curl -s -X POST https://softlight.orianna.ai/mcp/ \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Accept: application/json, text/event-stream' \
  -H "mcp-session-id: $SESSION" \
  -d @payload.json
```

Where `payload.json` is:
```json
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"update_iframe_element","arguments":{"project_id":"...","slot_id":"...","tunnel_id":"...","spec_url":"...","screenshot_urls":["..."],"preview_url":"..."}}}
```

Responses come back as SSE: `event: message\ndata: {...json...}`. Parse the JSON after `data: `.

Required arguments:
- `update_iframe_element`: `project_id`, `slot_id`, `tunnel_id`, `spec_url`, `screenshot_urls` (array of drive URLs), `preview_url` (single drive URL).
- `update_text_element`: `project_id`, `slot_id`, `text` (plain string, the caption).

```bash
curl -s -D /tmp/hdr.txt -X POST https://softlight.orianna.ai/mcp/ \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Accept: application/json, text/event-stream' \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize","params":{"protocolVersion":"2024-11-05","capabilities":{},"clientInfo":{"name":"a","version":"1"}}}' > /dev/null
SESSION=$(grep -i '^mcp-session-id' /tmp/hdr.txt | awk '{print $2}' | tr -d '\r')

# 2. Notify initialized
curl -s -X POST https://softlight.orianna.ai/mcp/ \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Accept: application/json, text/event-stream' \
  -H "mcp-session-id: $SESSION" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"notifications/initialized","params":{}}' > /dev/null

# 3. Call a tool (example: update_iframe_element)
curl -s -X POST https://softlight.orianna.ai/mcp/ \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Accept: application/json, text/event-stream' \
  -H "mcp-session-id: $SESSION" \
  -d @payload.json
```

Where `payload.json` is:
```json
{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"update_iframe_element","arguments":{"project_id":"...","slot_id":"...","tunnel_id":"...","spec_url":"...","screenshot_urls":["..."],"preview_url":"..."}}}
```

Responses come back as SSE: `event: message\ndata: {...json...}`. Parse the JSON after `data: `.

Required arguments:
- `update_iframe_element`: `project_id`, `slot_id`, `tunnel_id`, `spec_url`, `screenshot_urls` (array of drive URLs), `preview_url` (single drive URL).
- `update_text_element`: `project_id`, `slot_id`, `text` (plain string, the caption).
